profile.summary: Likely a very senior individual contributor in UX who sets direction on complex product areas, raises design quality, and influences product, engineering, and research partners. Often owns ambiguous problems, drives standards, and mentors others without formal people management. Scope varies a lot by company because "Expert UX" is not a standard title.

known_challenges.text: Influencing product decisions without direct authority, especially when delivery pressure outweighs user needs.
Balancing hands-on design work with expectations to mentor, review, and shape broader UX practice.
Working through vague ownership when the title signals seniority but the remit is not clearly defined.
Carrying the emotional load of being the person others look to for design judgment on hard calls.
Proving impact beyond polished deliverables, especially where UX outcomes are hard to measure.

recommended_question_themes.why: This level often succeeds or stalls based on how clearly they can own decisions across teams.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Influence with product and engineering peers
recommended_question_themes.why: Their impact depends on shaping direction through trust, evidence, and timing rather than authority.
Senior UX contributors are often expected to raise consistency and craft across more than their own work.
Informal leadership work can quietly consume time and energy if it is not acknowledged and managed.
They need support connecting design choices to user and business results, not just outputs.

terminology.meaning: Shared components and rules for consistent product design.
How the product behaves as people use it.
Designing so more people can use the product.
Visual view of a user's end-to-end experience.
Watching users try tasks to find friction.
Ongoing learning with users during product work.
Structured feedback on work to improve decisions.
Designer, product manager, and engineer working closely together.
Combining findings into patterns, insights, and implications.
Using AI tools to speed concept exploration.
Connecting user experience choices to product goals.
Getting key people to support a direction.
Reviewing work for quality, coherence, and risk.
Shaping decisions through trust, evidence, and communication.

listen_for: Whether they talk about outcomes and trade-offs, not just screens and flows.
How they describe influence across product and engineering when priorities conflict.
Whether mentoring and review work sounds energising, invisible, or draining.
Shifts from user evidence to opinion when defending decisions under pressure.
Whether they frame problems at team level or system level.
avoid: Assuming they want people management just because they are highly senior.
Reducing their value to mockups or speed of delivery.
Giving them broad design accountability without clear decision rights.
Treating mentoring and standards work as extra rather than core to the role.
